phase_metadata.title:
CSS Selectors and
Specificity
phase_metadata.description:
Mastering the art of targeting specific HTML
elements using advanced selectors, pseudo-classes, and understanding how the
browser resolves style conflicts.

learning_objectives:
Differentiate between Element, Class, and ID
selectors

Utilize combinators like descendant and child selectors to target
nested elements

Apply pseudo-classes like :hover and :nth-child for
interactive and structural styling

Enhance elements with pseudo-elements like
::before and ::after

Calculate CSS specificity to resolve style conflicts
effectively
Select elements based on their attributes
prerequisites:
Familiarity with HTML nesting and
attributes
introduction:
So far, you have styled tags like  or . But what
if you want to style only the third item in a list, or change a button's color
only when a user hovers over it? CSS Selectors are the 'search queries' of web
design. They allow you to pinpoint exactly which elements you want to style,
making your code precise and powerful.

concept_explanation.basic_selectors:
Element selectors target tags (h1). Class selectors target
groups (.btn). ID selectors target unique elements (#header).
concept_explanation.combinators:
These describe relationships between elements. A space (descendant) targets any
child/grandchild, while a '>' (child) targets only the direct child.
concept_explanation.pseudo_classes:
These define a special state of an element. For example,
':hover' applies when the mouse is over it, and ':nth-child(n)' targets an
element based on its position in a parent.
concept_explanation.pseudo_elements:
These allow you
to style specific parts of an element, like the first letter (::first-letter) or
inserting content before an element (::before).
concept_explanation.attribute_selectors:
Target
elements based on attributes like [type='text'] or [href^='https'].
concept_explanation.specificity_hierarchy:
When rules conflict, the browser uses a point system:
Inline (1000) > ID (100) > Class/Attribute/Pseudo-class (10) > Element (1).

breakdown_of_syntax.space: Represents any descendant, no matter how deep it is nested.
breakdown_of_syntax.greater_than: The child combinator; targets only elements one level down.
breakdown_of_syntax.content_property: Required for ::before and ::after to actually display something.

rules_and_key_points.rule_1:
IDs must be unique on a page; Classes can
be reused infinitely.
rules_and_key_points.rule_2:
The '' selector targets every single element
on the page.
rules_and_key_points.rule_3:
Pseudo-classes react to user interaction or document
structure.
rules_and_key_points.rule_4:
Pseudo-elements like ::before require the 'content'
property to work.
rules_and_key_points.rule_5:
Specificity: A single ID selector (#id) overrides
many class selectors.
rules_and_key_points.rule_6:
The !important flag overrides everything but
is considered a last resort.
rules_and_key_points.rule_7:
Group multiple selectors with a comma
(h1, h2, h3) to apply the same style.
rules_and_key_points.rule_8:
Selectors are read by the
browser from right to left (right-to-left parsing).
rules_and_key_points.rule_9:
Pseudo-classes
always start with one colon (:), pseudo-elements with two (::).
rules_and_key_points.rule_10: Specificity only matters when properties conflict.

code_example_1.title: Interactive List Styling
code_example_1.description:
Using pseudo-classes to
style a list based on interaction and position.

code_example_2.title: Advanced Combinators and Specificity
code_example_2.description:
Targeting
specific nested elements and demonstrating ::after.
real_world_use_cases:
Creating 'Zebra-striped' tables for better
readability using :nth-child(even).

Adding an arrow icon after every link in
a navigation menu using ::after.

Styling navigation links differently based
on which page is 'active'.

Targeting specifically password inputs to add an
icon or border color.

Highlighting rows in a dashboard when a user hovers
over them.
common_mistakes:
Using a space when you meant a '>': 'div p'
targets all paragraphs, 'div > p' only targets children.

Forgetting that IDs
should be unique; using the same ID twice will cause CSS to work but break
JavaScript.

Missing the 'content' property in ::before/::after, causing them
not to appear.

Assuming pseudo-elements like ::before can be applied to
self-closing tags like  (they cannot).

Relying too much on !important instead
of fixing specificity issues.
best_practices:
Use classes for styling
instead of IDs to keep specificity low and styles reusable.

Prefer the '>'
child combinator if you only want to style direct children to avoid accidental
nesting styles.

Organize selectors from general to specific in your
stylesheet.
Use pseudo-elements for decorative content to keep HTML clean.
Avoid deep nesting (e.g., div ul li a) as it makes CSS hard to maintain.

faqs.question:
What is the difference between :nth-child and
:nth-of-type?
faqs.answer:
:nth-child(2) looks for the second child of a parent
regardless of type. :nth-of-type(2) looks for the second occurrence of that
specific tag within the parent.

faqs.question:
Can I use multiple
pseudo-classes on one selector?
faqs.answer:
Yes, for example:
a:hover:first-child targets a link only if it is the first child AND it is being
hovered.

code_example_2.summary:
Phase 7 taught us how to precisely target HTML
elements. We moved from simple tag selectors to complex combinators, explored
the interactive world of pseudo-classes, and learned the mathematical point
system of specificity that browsers use to resolve styling conflicts.
key_takeaways: Classes are for groups; IDs are for unique individuals.
Combinators (space, >) define how deep to look for elements.
Specificity is a score (ID > Class > Element).

Attribute
selectors target metadata like type or href.

!important overrides everything
but should be avoided.
The '' selector is the most general (0 points).

Selectors are the logic
that connects HTML structure to CSS design.
code_example_2.preview_next_topic:
In
Phase 8, we will explore 'CSS Layout Basics'—learning the display property and
how to position boxes exactly where you want them on the screen.

questions_and_answers.question:
What is CSS Specificity and how is it
calculated?
questions_and_answers.answer:
CSS Specificity is the set of rules browsers use to
determine which style rule is applied when multiple rules target the same
element. It is calculated based on points: Inline styles = 1000, IDs = 100,
Classes/Pseudo-classes/Attributes = 10, and Elements/Pseudo-elements = 1.

questions_and_answers.question:
Explain the difference between
the descendant selector (space) and the child selector (>).
questions_and_answers.answer:
The
descendant selector (space) targets all specified elements inside a parent,
regardless of how deeply nested they are. The child selector (>) only targets
elements that are direct children (exactly one level down) of the parent.

questions_and_answers.question:
Write a selector that changes
the color of a link to red only when it is being hovered AND it is inside a div
with the class 'sidebar'.

How would you select every
even row in a table and give it a light gray background?

Identify the bug: A student uses img::before {
content: 'Image'; } but nothing appears. Why?
questions_and_answers.answer:
Pseudo-elements like
::before and ::after do not work on 'replaced elements' or self-closing tags
like , , or  because these elements do not have a content area where the
pseudo-element can be inserted.

questions_and_answers.question:
A student wrote #nav .link { color: blue; } and later wrote nav
.link { color: red; }. Which color will the link be and why?
questions_and_answers.answer:
The
link will be blue. This is because the first selector includes an ID (#nav),
giving it a higher specificity score (110) compared to the second selector which
only uses an element and a class (11).

questions_and_answers.question:
Create a CSS rule that adds a double-quote symbol before and after
every paragraph with the class 'quote'.

Write a selector to target only input elements that
have a 'required' attribute and are currently in focus.

multiple_choice_questions.question:
Which selector has
the highest specificity?
Which pseudo-class targets the very first child of a parent?

What is the output of: div p { color: red; } applied to

Hello

?

options.A:
Text is red
because it's a descendant
options.B:
Text is black because it's not a direct
child

multiple_choice_questions.question:
Find the bug: .my-class:hover::before { color:
red; } (Assuming content is missing)

options.B: It needs a 'content' property to be visible
options.C:
Hover cannot
be combined with before

multiple_choice_questions.question:
Find the bug: p > span i { color: gold; } in

Text Gold

options.B: It should be p > span > i for descendant

multiple_choice_questions.question:
Complete the code to target links
starting with 'https': a[href___='https']

Complete the code for a double-colon pseudo-element:
p____first-line { font-weight: bold; }
What is the best approach for styling elements that appear multiple times?

options.A: Give each one a unique ID
